title: Sultan of Brunei

infobox.value: [Istana Nurul Iman](/wiki/Istana_Nurul_Iman "Istana Nurul Iman"), [Bandar Seri Begawan](/wiki/Bandar_Seri_Begawan "Bandar Seri Begawan")
Portrait | Name | Reign start | Reign end | Notes

| [Muhammad Shah](/wiki/Muhammad_Shah_of_Brunei "Muhammad Shah of Brunei") / Awang Alak Betatar | 1363/1368[[3]](#cite_note-Elisseeff2000-3) | 1402 | He founded modern Brunei in the early 1360s after converting to Islam. He established a new administrative centre near the [Brunei River](/wiki/Brunei_River "Brunei River"), enhancing his control over the region. Under his rule, Brunei expanded its territories by seizing land from the declining [Majapahit Empire](/wiki/Majapahit_Empire "Majapahit Empire") and subduing local vassals. His conversion to Islam, motivated by a marriage alliance with the [Sultan of Johor](/wiki/Sultan_of_Johor "Sultan of Johor"), marked a significant shift from a Hindu-Buddhist monarchy to an Islamic sultanate. He also engaged in diplomatic relations with [Ming China](/wiki/Ming_China "Ming China").

| [Abdul Majid Hassan](/wiki/Abdul_Majid_Hassan "Abdul Majid Hassan") / Maharaja Karna | 1402 | 1408 | In 1405, he sent envoys to China to pay homage to the [Yongle Emperor](/wiki/Yongle_Emperor "Yongle Emperor"), receiving an imperial mandate and seal in return. His visit to [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") in 1408 marked him as the first foreign king to attend the imperial court under the emperor. After spending over a month at the court, he fell ill and died on 19 October 1408, at the age of 28. His final wishes included being buried in China, and he was interred at the [Tomb of the King of Boni](/wiki/Tomb_of_the_King_of_Boni "Tomb of the King of Boni") in Nanjing, where his grave was later rediscovered and designated a historical site.

| [Ahmad](/wiki/Sultan_Ahmad_of_Brunei "Sultan Ahmad of Brunei") / Awang Pateh Berbai | 1408 | 1425 | During his reign, he established diplomatic ties with Ming China, undertaking a tribute mission to the court of Yongle Emperor, where he personally pledged loyalty and presented gifts. This mission positioned him as the first southern ruler to formally engage in such tributes, enhancing Brunei's standing with the Ming dynasty.

| [Sharif Ali](/wiki/Sharif_Ali "Sharif Ali") / Sultan Barkat (Blessed Sultan) | 1425 | 1432 | Previously the [Sharif of Mecca](/wiki/Sharif_of_Mecca "Sharif of Mecca") of the [Mamluk Sultanate](/wiki/Mamluk_Sultanate_(Cairo) "Mamluk Sultanate (Cairo)") and has no direct genealogical relation to the predecessor, but was selected as he was the son-in-law of his predecessor and was well versed in Islam.

| [Sulaiman](/wiki/Sulaiman_of_Brunei "Sulaiman of Brunei") | 1432 | 1485 | He promoted the spread of Islam while overseeing the construction of [Kota Batu](/wiki/Kota_Batu,_Brunei "Kota Batu, Brunei"). His reign was notable for the fair application of the Brunel Legal Code, as evidenced by his stern punishment of royal relatives who violated Islamic teachings, reflecting his commitment to justice. He abdicated in 1485 in favour of his son Bolkiah, taking on the title *Paduka Seri Begawan Sultan*.

| [Bolkiah](/wiki/Bolkiah "Bolkiah") / Nakhoda Ragam (The Singing Captain) | 1485 | 1524 | His reign marked Brunei's golden age, during which he significantly expanded the empire's territory to include present-day Sarawak, Sabah, [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila"), and the [Sulu Archipelago](/wiki/Sulu_Archipelago "Sulu Archipelago"). His military successes against rival states like the [Sultanate of Sulu](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Sulu "Sultanate of Sulu") and Saludang enhanced Brunei's influence and wealth, facilitating the spread of Islam in the region. His interactions with European explorers, particularly in 1521 during the [Magellan expedition](/wiki/Magellan_expedition "Magellan expedition"), highlighted Brunei's diplomatic strength and cultural richness.

| [Abdul Kahar](/wiki/Abdul_Kahar "Abdul Kahar") | 1524 | 1530 | He established trade relations with the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"), including a friendship and trade agreement with his predecessor after Portuguese explorer [Jorge de Menezes](/wiki/Jorge_de_Menezes "Jorge de Menezes") visited Brunei in 1526. During his reign, he undertook naval expeditions to expand Brunei's territories, reaching regions such as [Palawan](/wiki/Palawan "Palawan"), [Sulu](/wiki/Sulu "Sulu"), and [Luzon](/wiki/Luzon "Luzon"), and introduced a new currency called *pitis*. He abdicated in 1530.

| [Saiful Rijal](/wiki/Saiful_Rijal "Saiful Rijal") | 1530 | 1581 | His reign was marked by the [Castilian War](/wiki/Castilian_War "Castilian War"), which began after the Spanish accused Brunei of supporting Muslim missionary activities in the Philippines. In retaliation for Brunei's disregard of their orders, the Spaniards attacked and briefly captured the capital in 1578. He relocated the capital to Saragua and strengthened the Brunei military. After the Spaniards retreated later that year, he oversaw the reconstruction of the capital.
| [Shah Brunei](/wiki/Shah_Brunei "Shah Brunei") | 1581 | 1582 | Eldest son of [Sultan Saiful Rijal](/wiki/Saiful_Rijal "Saiful Rijal")

| [Muhammad Hasan](/wiki/Muhammad_Hasan_of_Brunei "Muhammad Hasan of Brunei") | 1582 | 1598 | He enhanced infrastructure with the construction of palaces and bridges to improve governance. He appointed new Wazirs, defended Sunni Islam in the Philippines, and led military campaigns against the Milau clan in Sarawak. His reign fostered strong ties with the [Sultanate of Pahang](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Pahang "Sultanate of Pahang"), and he influenced legal codes through his establishment of comprehensive [canons](/wiki/Canon_law "Canon law") addressing daily life.

| [Abdul Jalilul Akbar](/wiki/Abdul_Jalilul_Akbar "Abdul Jalilul Akbar") | 1598 | 1659 | During his rule, he strengthened ties with the [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), establishing a peace treaty and facilitating trade between Brunei and the Philippines. His reign also saw visits from notable traders, including Dutch trader [Oliver Van Noort](/wiki/Oliver_Van_Noort "Oliver Van Noort") in 1600 and English businessman Sir [Hendry Middleton](/wiki/Henry_Middleton_(captain)

([Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"): *Sultan Brunei*, [Jawi](/wiki/Jawi_alphabet "Jawi alphabet"): سلطان بروني‎) is the monarchical [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") of [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") and [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") in his capacity as [prime minister of Brunei](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_Brunei "Prime minister of Brunei").[[1]](#cite_note-official-1) Since independence from the [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") in 1984, only one sultan has reigned, though the royal institution dates back to the 14th century.[[2]](#cite_note-list-2) The sultan of Brunei can be thought of as synonymous with the ruling [House of Bolkiah](/wiki/House_of_Bolkiah "House of Bolkiah"), with generations being traced from the first sultan, [Muhammad Shah](/wiki/Muhammad_Shah_of_Brunei "Muhammad Shah of Brunei"), temporarily interrupted by the thirteenth sultan, [Abdul Hakkul Mubin](/wiki/Abdul_Hakkul_Mubin "Abdul Hakkul Mubin"), who in turn was deposed by a member of the House of Bolkiah. The sultan's full title is **His Majesty The Sultan and [Yang di-Pertuan](/wiki/Yang_di-Pertuan_Negara "Yang di-Pertuan Negara") of Brunei Darussalam**.[[1]](#cite_note-official-1) The Sultan of Brunei was also regarded as the Sultan of Sarawak, Labuan and Sabah before the Europeans took over the three territories.

The **Sultan of Brunei** ([Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"): *Sultan Brunei*, [Jawi](/wiki/Jawi_alphabet "Jawi alphabet"): سلطان بروني‎) is the monarchical [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") of [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") and [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") in his capacity as [prime minister of Brunei](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_Brunei "Prime minister of Brunei").[[1]](#cite_note-official-1) Since independence from the [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") in 1984, only one sultan has reigned, though the royal institution dates back to the 14th century.[[2]](#cite_note-list-2) The sultan of Brunei can be thought of as synonymous with the ruling [House of Bolkiah](/wiki/House_of_Bolkiah "House of Bolkiah"), with generations being traced from the first sultan, [Muhammad Shah](/wiki/Muhammad_Shah_of_Brunei "Muhammad Shah of Brunei"), temporarily interrupted by the thirteenth sultan, [Abdul Hakkul Mubin](/wiki/Abdul_Hakkul_Mubin "Abdul Hakkul Mubin"), who in turn was deposed by a member of the House of Bolkiah. The sultan's full title is **His Majesty The Sultan and [Yang di-Pertuan](/wiki/Yang_di-Pertuan_Negara "Yang di-Pertuan Negara") of Brunei Darussalam**.[[1]](#cite_note-official-1) The Sultan of Brunei was also regarded as the Sultan of Sarawak, Labuan and Sabah before the Europeans took over the three territories.

## Uncertainties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_sultans_of_Brunei&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Uncertainties")]

The earliest historical record of the [Sultans of Brunei](/wiki/List_of_Sultans_of_Brunei "List of Sultans of Brunei") is not clearly known due to the poor early documentation of Brunei history. In addition there has been an effort to [retroactively](/wiki/Historical_negationism "Historical negationism") *Islamise* the history, with the "official history" not matching up with verifiable foreign sources [[4]](#cite_note-royalark-4) The *[Batu Tarsilah](/wiki/Batu_Tarsilah "Batu Tarsilah")*, the genealogical record of the kings of Brunei, was not started until 1807. Therefore, much of the interpretation of history relied on earlier Chinese sources and legends. It seems that the early Sultanate of Brunei was dependent on Chinese support,[[3]](#cite_note-Elisseeff2000-3)[[5]](#cite_note-missing-5)[[6]](#cite_note-history-6) and perhaps early Sultans were of Chinese origin.[[3]](#cite_note-Elisseeff2000-3) Furthermore, the earliest Sultans may have been practicing the Hindu or Buddhist religions, with early names indicating this origin.[[4]](#cite_note-royalark-4)

In recent years, Brunei's [historiography](/wiki/Historiography "Historiography") has been reexamined due to the disparity between the official regnal years of the sultans and the historical facts that are currently available. An unnamed [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language") trader who resided in Brunei before to 1590 made the first recorded attempt to document the country's history; John S. Carroll subsequently published his work. By claiming that the Bruneis were invaders who subjugated the indigenous [Bisayahs](/wiki/Bisaya_(Borneo) "Bisaya (Borneo)"), this merchant distinguished clearly between accepted customs and empirical evidence. Although traditional belief credited this migration to Sultan Yuso, who supposedly came in Brunei after orchestrating a major exodus from the Malay-speaking city of Cauin, he suggested that the Bruneians moved from the [Mekong Delta](/wiki/Mekong_Delta "Mekong Delta") in the late [17th century](/wiki/17th_century "17th century"). The trader said that Sultan Yuso conquered the Bisayahs, found riches, and was then granted power by the [Chinese Emperor](/wiki/Chinese_Emperor "Chinese Emperor"). Even while his story is full with regional [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore"), it also makes reference to actual monarchs like Sultan Soliman and Sultan Abdul Kahar, which helps establish him as a reliable historian.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

The next important character in Brunei's history is Datuk Imam Ya'akub, who wrote between 1680 and 1690, under Sultan Aliuddin's rule. He put together a *Silsilah* that served as the foundation for the initial sections of two books that [Amin Sweeney](/wiki/Amin_Sweeney "Amin Sweeney") revised. Regretfully, these passages contradict each other, casting doubt on Ya'akub's actual identification of Sultan Ahmad and whether he was referring to the Chinese mandarin [Ong Sum Ping](/wiki/Ong_Sum_Ping "Ong Sum Ping") or Sultan Muhammad's brother. This suggests that the text has been corrupted over time. Sir [Hugh Low](/wiki/Hugh_Low "Hugh Low") stands out among the European explorers of Southeast Asia due to his intense interest in history. Prior to [Donald Brown](/wiki/Donald_Brown_(anthropologist) "Donald Brown (anthropologist)")'s publication of his work in 1970, his 1880 submission to the [Journal of the Straits Branch of the Royal Asiatic Society](/wiki/Journal_of_the_Straits_Branch_of_the_Royal_Asiatic_Society "Journal of the Straits Branch of the Royal Asiatic Society") was the first significant modern attempt to record Brunei's history. Despite having access to Pengiran Kasuma's *Silsilah* and other records, Low was unable to date Brunei's historical chronology since it lacked dates and allusions to outside events. Given the lengthy reigns of multiple sultans at that time, he surmised that the adoption of Islam and the founding of the dynasty most likely took place about 1403, but they may have happened earlier.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

Due to differing opinions on the number of sultans mentioned in the *Silsilah*, it has been difficult to determine the exact date of Muhammad Shah. This disparity calls into doubt not only the notable lack of documents from 1690 to 1790, but also co-reigning sultans, competing monarchs, and lesser-known individuals, like the newborn [Olivier van Noort](/wiki/Olivier_van_Noort "Olivier van Noort") cited. Sweeney's critical edition of the [London](/wiki/London "London") manuscripts of the *Silsilah* was supposed to further historical study in Brunei, but in the end, it didn't because the inconsistencies in the texts made researchers hesitant to conduct more in-depth research. A significant contribution was made in 1970 by Brown, who collected several historical views in his study *Brunei: The Structure and History of a Bornean Malay Sultanate*. He brought up [Paul Pelliot](/wiki/Paul_Pelliot "Paul Pelliot")'s association between Mahmud Shah and Ma-ha-mo-sha, implying that Muhammad Shah's rule may have begun as early as the [14th century](/wiki/14th_century "14th century"). But Brown also cited P. Manuel Teixeira's analysis of [Rui de Brito Patalim](/w/index.php?title=Rui_de_Brito_Patalim&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rui de Brito Patalim (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rui_de_Brito_Patalim "pt:Rui de Brito Patalim")]'s correspondence, which suggested that there were two different Bruneis: a [pagan](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism") and a Muslim. Local academics opposed this idea, arguing that Brunei was established as a Muslim kingdom under Muhammad Shah from the beginning, so confirming the idea that it has always been a shining example of Islam in Southeast Asia.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

An inscription discovered in 1979 indicated that [Abdul Majid Hassan](/wiki/Abdul_Majid_Hassan "Abdul Majid Hassan"), the son of Muhammad Shah, died in [A.H.](/wiki/Hijri_year "Hijri year") 440/[A.D.](/wiki/A.D. "A.D.") 1048. This would have placed Brunei as the first Muslim kingdom in Southeast Asia and dated Muhammad Muhammad's rule to the early 17th century. However, experts eventually refuted this notion. The release of an erroneous family tree that featured the fake Abdul Majid dashed hopes for a more academic study of Brunei's history, which had been raised by the establishment of the [Brunei History Centre](/wiki/Brunei_History_Centre "Brunei History Centre") in 1984. A new history curriculum that mandated extensive textbooks in 1987 heightened the drive for clarity in Brunei's history and raised serious concerns about the reign of Muhammad Shah, [Bendahara Sakam](/wiki/Bendahara_Sakam "Bendahara Sakam")'s accomplishments, and the circumstances surrounding Muhammad Aliuddin's death in 1690. In 1988, these important historical questions were still being debated.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)
